items.title: Laser-based 3D imaging system enables precise detection and quantification of methane leakage
items.summary: A research team led by Prof. Zhang Zhirong from the Hefei Institutes of Physical Science of the Chinese Academy of Sciences has developed a high-performance laser-based three-dimensional methane gas cloud imaging telemetry system that enabled visualization of microleakages, accurate location of leak

items.title: New millisecond pulsar discovered with the Murchison Widefield Array
items.summary: Using the Murchison Widefield Array (MWA), astronomers have discovered a new millisecond pulsar as part of the ongoing Southern-sky MWA Rapid Two-metre (SMART) survey. The discovery is reported in a research paper published June 17 on the arXiv preprint server. The work has also been accepted for pu

items.title: New workflow transforms nonfunctional protein scaffolds into active enzymes
items.summary: Enzymes are regarded as the key to sustainable chemistry. Despite major advances in protein design, creating artificial enzymes from scratch has so far remained a grand challenge. A research team at the University of Bayreuth, in collaboration with scientists from the University of Ottawa, has now d

items.title: How everyone pays the cost for patents on seeds, and private companies get rich from keeping them secret
items.summary: The United States is one of only a handful of countries that allows companies to hold patents on plant varieties. As a result, a small number of corporations can—and do—suppress competition in the seed industry, stifle innovation, and turn taxpayer subsidies intended for farmers into corporate profi

items.title: Non-Hermitian geometry reveals when quantum amplification depends only on start and end points
items.summary: In quantum mechanics, the geometry of quantum states has emerged as a powerful framework for understanding phenomena ranging from electrical conductivity to superconductivity. One research direction aims to extend these geometric concepts to non-Hermitian quantum mechanics—where systems can exchange

items.title: Research team cuts cost of building reconstituted cell-free systems by 95%
items.summary: A research team led by Professor Joongoo Lee in the Department of Chemical Engineering at POSTECH (Pohang University of Science and Technology) has developed an automated, modular method for assembling reconstituted cell-free systems, which are test-tube systems that can produce proteins outside liv

items.title: Coastal and estuarine carbon removal technique may backfire when pushed too far
items.summary: Scientists investigating a proposed way to remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere using seawater have found that adding too much alkalinity to neutralize acids can trigger chemical reactions that undermine the process.

items.title: Saturday Citations: Predicting earthquakes; two types of water; observing event horizons
items.summary: Howdy, pards, here's a quick roundup of the week's science news: Moose, previously thought to be a transplanted species, are actually native to Colorado. A digital twin of a two-year-old child's brain revealed neural signatures linked to autism. And a new gel treatment for severed spi

items.title: Lamprey brain atlas reveals 450-million-year blueprint of vertebrate brains
items.summary: What did the very first complex vertebrate brain look like? To find out, scientists turned to an unlikely time traveler: the lamprey, a jawless, eel-like fish whose body plan has barely changed in roughly 360 million years.

items.title: Swiss glaciers facing drastic loss from heat wave: Expert
items.summary: Swiss glaciers are set to lose an enormous amount of ice due to the heat wave battering Europe, the head of Glacier Monitoring in Switzerland (GLAMOS) told AFP.

items.title: New driving model predicts split-second crash avoidance with humanlike accuracy
items.summary: Scientists at Delft University of Technology, in collaboration with Waymo, have developed a new model that predicts with high accuracy how human drivers respond to dangerous traffic situations. For the first time, different types of collision avoidance behavior are combined into a single model. The

items.title: The bond between humans and dogs remains remarkably consistent across societies, cross-cultural study reveals
items.summary: A new study by an international research team led by Friedrich Schiller University Jena and the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology (Leipzig) has revealed striking similarities in the way humans and dogs interact in very different societies. The research is published in the journal Sc

items.title: UK sets new June temperature record for third day in a row: Met Office
items.summary: The UK broke the record for a June temperature for the third day in a row on Friday, the Met Office weather agency said, as a sweltering heat wave strained schools and hospitals and drove down business.

items.title: Primate evolution kept aging rates stable for 25 million years despite lifespan gaps
items.summary: Biologists group animals with similar traits into broad categories called orders. Despite their similarities, animal species in the same order can have very different average lifespans.

items.title: Farmers fear drought as Italy's longest river runs dry
items.summary: Seawater is seeping into Italy's longest river as the waterway starts to run dry in the heat wave, hitting a farming heartland that produces the milk for Parmesan cheese.

items.title: Germany sees hottest temperature on record of 41.3C: Weather service
items.summary: Germany saw its highest temperature ever recorded Friday at 41.3°C (106.3°F), according to preliminary weather service data, as the country braces for the possibility of even higher temperatures over the weekend.

items.title: A large, harmless asteroid will zip past Earth this weekend
items.summary: A large asteroid will zip past Earth this weekend, but don't worry: It poses no danger.

items.title: Thawing ground, future questions: Decoding Arctic climate in a lab
items.summary: In a Penn State lab, a small cylinder of soil sits wired with sensors, slowly cooling as it mimics conditions thousands of miles away.

items.title: Two humpback whales set records swimming between Australia and Brazil
items.summary: Scientists have spotted two humpback whales that made separate, record-breaking crossings between Australia and Brazil.

items.title: AI nas dokazano zaglupljuje
items.summary: GUBITEK nekih sposobnosti ozbiljno zabrinjava.

items.title: Participatory theater helps young people become active citizens
items.summary: In Estonian schools, social studies classes provide a thorough education on what democracy is and what it means to be a citizen. However, knowledge alone is not enough to foster active citizenship or reduce the differences in civic skills between Estonian- and Russian-speaking young people. A doctor

items.title: By co-operating with local governments, organizations, universities have a vital role to play in tackling climate change
items.summary: Governments, corporations and other institutions must all play constructive roles in mitigating the impacts of climate change. Universities, too, can and must help with that effort.

items.title: Investigative interviews are key to solving crimes—should AI be helping police with their inquiries?
items.summary: Investigative interviewing—the process of obtaining accurate and complete accounts from victims, witnesses and suspects—is the lifeblood of the criminal justice system.

items.title: Vulnerable butterfly recorded in the Botanical Garden at Uppsala
items.summary: The Botanical Garden in Uppsala was recently visited by animal ecology researchers, who conducted a BioBlitz to find, identify and record as many insects as possible in the Botanical Garden. One of the finds was an endangered butterfly called the Small Blue, Cupido minimus. It is abundant in one of

items.title: Uranus, Neptune may be magma worlds, not ice giants
items.summary: Uranus and Neptune remain two of the most mysterious objects in the solar system, primarily because they have been visited only by NASA's Voyager 2 spacecraft in 1986 and 1989, respectively. Their "ice giant" moniker comes from longstanding hypotheses that their interiors are comprised of an ic

items.title: Proactive employees with high emotional intelligence do a better job, study finds
items.summary: In many organizations, large hierarchical gaps exist within work teams, raising the question of how frontline employees can strive for upward mobility in their careers. A recent study by the Department of Psychology at Lingnan University finds that employees who proactively seek opportunities, are w

items.title: Coal pollution reaches one of Earth's most remote mountain regions
items.summary: The Himalayas are often seen as one of Earth's great natural barriers, separating the heavily populated and industrialized regions of South Asia from the remote Tibetan Plateau. But new research, published in Geophysical Research Letters, suggests that this mountain range is not an impenetrable

items.title: Why Ho Chi Minh City's pollution sources may have been misread for years
items.summary: Biomass burning, including the combustion of wood, charcoal and agricultural residues, is a major source of PM2.5, a fine particulate matter that degrades air quality and poses risks to human health. Much of this pollution is tracked by looking at levels of levoglucosan, a chemical that is formed wh

items.title: Clean crystal surface lets single molecules hit ultimate quantum limit
items.summary: Scientists at the Max Planck Institute for the Science of Light (MPL) have developed a technique for interrogating molecules on surfaces with spectroscopic precision, thereby reaching the ultimate quantum limit for the first time. With their findings, published in Science, the researchers open new o

items.title: Ocean warming above 1.5°C triggered year-round marine disruption across globe, study shows
items.summary: Researchers at King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST) led one of the first global assessments of how marine ecosystems responded during the first year when global temperatures temporarily exceeded 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.

items.title: Japan's small cities may face higher care burdens under the compact city policy
items.summary: As populations decline and age across the developed world, compact city strategies, which oversee the consolidation of urban facilities and guide residents toward transit-served hubs, have become mainstream policies. Yet most evidence about their benefits comes from cities that are already compact.

items.title: ROS-producing enzymes guide plant cell division and tissue patterning, gene-editing study shows
items.summary: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced naturally during cellular metabolism often cause oxidative damage to cells. However, these molecules also play an important role in normal cellular signaling. While ROS are established as essential signaling molecules in various organisms, their precise role in

items.title: Pegasus launch to deploy LINK for months‑long orbit boost of aging Swift
items.summary: A mission to raise the orbit of NASA's Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory is poised for launch no earlier than Tuesday, June 30, at 6:23 a.m. EDT (10:23 p.m. UTC+12), from Kwajalein Atoll, part of the Republic of the Marshall Islands in the South Pacific Ocean.

items.title: Plasma and graphene combine to protect metal surfaces from corrosion
items.summary: Plasma is an ionized gas, often referred to as the fourth state of matter. Plasmas, which are created artificially by applying energy to a gas, are found in the fluorescent tubes that illuminate kitchens. However, they have many other possible applications, such as the production of graphene.

items.title: NASA Announces Winners for 2026 Human Lander Challenge
items.summary: NASA has announced the top student-developed solutions for environmental control and life support systems in future crewed lunar landers from participants in the 2026 Human Lander Challenge. The announcement marks the culmination of months of research by university teams working to advance technolog

items.title: NASA Announces Winners for 2026 Human Lander Challenge
items.summary: NASA has announced the top student-developed solutions for environmental control and life support systems in future crewed lunar landers from participants in the 2026 Human Lander Challenge. The announcement marks the culmination of months of research by university teams working to advance technolog

items.title: Glass cells of atoms offer a new path to smarter, cheaper sensors
items.summary: More accurate navigation systems and improved wireless communications may not come from traditional electronics, but rather from atoms. Researchers at Penn State and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) have developed a new way to build tinier, smarter glass sensors filled with

items.title: Hubble spies ancient 'Chandelier Cluster' forming stars in two bursts
items.summary: The subject of today's NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescope image is an ancient inhabitant of our galaxy. This sparkling scene features a globular cluster: a collection of tens of thousands to millions of stars, all tightly bound together under the influence of gravity. There are more than 150 globu

items.title: Beetle-like borings in 70-million-year-old titanosaur fossils reshape Lo Hueco fossil story
items.summary: Traces or perforations caused by living organisms after an animal's death can be found on various dinosaur bone remains. These perforations, known as bioerosion structures, provide information that helps us understand relationships between living organisms in the past, reconstruct palaeoecosyst

items.title: NASA's PACE mission studies smoke and fires
items.summary: With the North American fire season underway, and a record number of acres already burned nationwide, NASA's Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, and ocean Ecosystem (PACE) satellite's three instruments are observing vegetation precursors to fires, along with plumes of smoke and their movement. These

items.title: Unknown 4,000-year-old stone circle in Belfast uncovered by archaeologists
items.summary: Archaeologists have uncovered an unknown stone circle that dates back at least 4,000 years to the Late Neolithic or Early Bronze Age and was most likely used for ritual activities.

items.title: How atomic defects can program carbon quantum dots for future light-based technologies
items.summary: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are tiny carbon-based nanomaterials that have attracted increasing attention as environmentally friendly alternatives to conventional heavy-metal quantum dots. They are lightweight, photostable and potentially biocompatible, and their light absorption and emission properti

items.title: NASA Tests New Refuel Device for Future In-Space Refueling Missions
items.summary: For NASA’s next generation of deep space exploration missions, spacecraft may need to refuel in Earth orbit before pushing farther into the solar system. Similar to how a gas pump needs a nozzle to fit your fuel tank, future spacecraft could require a special device in order to fill up prior to depa

items.title: Evidence identifies ancient Aboriginal mining in the Riverland
items.summary: Flinders University researchers, in partnership with the River Murray and Mallee Aboriginal Corporation, have found evidence that points to 7,000 years of Aboriginal mining of stone at Sugarloaf Hill in South Australia's Riverland. The dating formed part of the first detailed investigation into

items.title: Apple rootstock response varies to threshold water management during 6 weeks of progressing drought
items.summary: As drought and water uncertainty put increasing pressure on orchard systems, researchers at the USDA Agricultural Research Service's Appalachian Fruit Research Station in Kearneysville, West Virginia, launched a study in response to strong grower interest in how modern apple rootstocks hold up

items.title: Far-red radiation and elevated CO₂ boost biomass accumulation in young leaf lettuce indoors
items.summary: A new study from scientists at Purdue University reports that far-red radiation, in combination with increased carbon dioxide (CO₂) concentration, significantly enhances biomass production in young leaf lettuce grown under controlled environments, offering practical guidance for indoor farming opera

items.title: Rising heat and humidity challenge energy-efficient data center cooling worldwide
items.summary: Reliable operation of data centers has become essential to nearly all sectors of modern society, including health care, education, government services, power grid operation, banking, defense and disaster relief. New research published in Scientific Reports, led by University of Hawai'i (UH) at

items.title: How coworkers shape careers: Learning and competition pull in opposite directions
items.summary: New research, published by the Rockwool Foundation Berlin (RFBerlin), shows that co-workers can have powerful—but opposing—effects on the careers of young workers. While some colleagues foster learning and long-term success, others may hinder career progression through direct competition. The study

items.title: May 2024 superstorm drew most ring current ions from Earth, not solar wind, research reveals
items.summary: In May 2024, auroras were observed at unusually low latitudes across the globe, lighting up skies that rarely see such displays. Inside Earth's magnetosphere, the region of space surrounding our planet and dominated by its intrinsic magnetic field, something significant was finally observed.

items.title: Three ways climate action can be more inclusive for 1.3 billion disabled people
items.summary: Imagine a global political summit that shapes the future of our planet, where one of the most populous countries in the world does not have a voice. This may seem unlikely, but currently 1.3 billion disabled people (nearly the population of China) do not have formal representation at policy talks he

items.title: Novel crystal strategy delivers near-perfect zero thermal expansion from 11 K to 893 K
items.summary: Almost every material expands when heated. Well-known examples include railroad tracks and concrete roadways, which feature visible expansion gaps to accommodate this effect. However, thermal expansion poses a far more acute challenge for extremely precise technologies, such as lasers and semiconduc

items.title: How NASA taught four astronauts to read the moon
items.summary: How do you teach someone to look at the moon? Not glance at it, the way we all have on a clear night, but truly read it, the way a geologist reads a hillside. That was the challenge NASA set itself before Artemis II, because when Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen swung ar

items.title: An iron-driven chain reaction may trigger mass death of harmful algae blooms
items.summary: Over recent decades, harmful algal blooms have become increasingly common. These blooms often consist of bacteria called "cyanobacteria" in freshwater ecosystems. They can produce debilitating toxins, suffocate marine life by depleting oxygen in the water, and make water unsafe for drinking.

items.title: Partners, NASA Ready for June Launch of Swift Boost Mission
items.summary: A mission to raise the orbit of NASA’s Swift observatory is poised for launch June 30.
